ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for commercially sweetened canned jasmine green tea RTD. Mapped to broader 'tea beverage' (FOODON_03301099) as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code AN0KN covers tea-based beverages broadly; no specific code for sweetened canned jasmine green tea RTD exists in the published FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for commercially sweetened canned jasmine green tea beverage.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard 300ml can, equivalent to approximately 300g, is the typical single-serve portion as sold in Singapore fast food outlets and convenience stores.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Branded Foods: Fuze Tea Jasmine Green Tea / closest RTD sweetened green tea analogue
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input per_100g values (energy 30 kcal, carbohydrate 7.5g, sugar 7.5g, protein 0g, fat 0g, sodium 5mg) are consistent with the Fuze Tea Jasmine Green Tea product label and align with USDA FoodData Central branded RTD sweetened green tea entries (e.g., FDC ID 1104963 — Fuze Tea, Green Tea with Honey). Values adopted from product label data cross-validated against USDA Branded Foods. Protein and fat are confirmed zero by product formulation (water, sugar, tea extract base). Cholesterol and saturated fat are confirmed zero. Trans fat confirmed zero. Dietary fibre confirmed zero for this beverage type.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database — sweetened tea / sugar-sweetened beverage analogues
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Fuze Tea Jasmine Green Tea in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 60 (medium) based on: (1) sucrose GI ~65 (University of Sydney GI Database, direct entry); (2) sugar-sweetened beverages with similar sugar concentration (7–8g/100g) cluster around GI 58–65 in peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008); (3) the beverage is essentially a dilute sucrose solution with negligible fibre or fat to attenuate glycaemic response. Glycemic load per 300ml serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (60 × 22.5) / 100 = 13.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage (7.5g sugar/100g); added sugar independently raises blood pressure via insulin-driven sodium retention
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this beverage: at 7.5g sugar/100g it qualifies as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB), and a 300ml can delivers ~22.5g of added sugar which promotes insulin-mediated sodium retention and elevated blood pressure independent of its low sodium content (5mg/100g); choose unsweetened jasmine green tea instead.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High sugar load (7.5g/100g, all added sugar) with zero dietary fibre; rapid glycaemic spike from liquid sugar
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this beverage: a 300ml can delivers approximately 22.5g of rapidly absorbed added sugar with no fibre buffering, producing a significant glycaemic load of ~13.5 and rapid postprandial glucose elevation; unsweetened jasmine green tea is the recommended alternative as EGCG catechins may support insulin sensitivity.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Regular SSB consumption linked to elevated triglycerides, visceral adiposity, and increased CVD risk; zero saturated fat and sodium are mitigating factors
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals managing cardiovascular disease risk should limit or avoid this beverage: habitual consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages at this sugar concentration (7.5g/100g) is associated with elevated serum triglycerides and increased CVD risk per AHA dietary guidelines; the absence of saturated fat and low sodium are positive attributes but do not offset the SSB risk at regular consumption.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar at 7.5g/100g promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine production, overriding the anti-inflammatory EGCG benefit of green tea
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this sweetened beverage as the added sugar load (22.5g per 300ml can) promotes inflammatory mediators that outweigh the anti-inflammatory EGCG catechin content; unsweetened jasmine green tea provides genuine anti-inflammatory benefit without the sugar-driven inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fuze Tea Jasmine Green Tea (Canned, 300ml) is a commercially manufactured, ready-to-drink (RTD) sweetened tea beverage produced by The Coca-Cola Company and widely sold in Singapore at fast food outlets, convenience stores, and supermarkets. The drink is made from water, sugar, green tea extract, and jasmine flavouring, with ascorbic acid (vitamin C) added as an antioxidant and citric acid for tartness. It is served chilled and delivers a light, floral jasmine aroma with a mild green tea flavour and a moderately sweet taste profile. Per 100ml, the beverage provides 30 kcal, 7.5g of carbohydrate (entirely from added sugar), and negligible protein and fat. A standard 300ml can delivers approximately 90 kcal and 22.5g of added sugar. Protein and fat are naturally zero as this is a water-and-sugar-based tea extract beverage with no protein or fat-containing ingredients. Clinically, this beverage is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) and is unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease due to its high added sugar content and rapid glycaemic impact. The estimated glycemic index is 60 (medium), yielding a glycemic load of approximately 13.5 per 300ml serving. While green tea EGCG catechins have documented anti-inflammatory and insulin-sensitising properties, these benefits are substantially negated in the sweetened commercial form. Unsweetened jasmine green tea is the clinically preferred alternative for health-conscious consumers.

confidence_reason: Per-100g nutrient values are consistent with the Fuze Tea Jasmine Green Tea product label and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central branded RTD sweetened green tea entries. Protein and fat are confirmed zero by product formulation. GI is estimated from the closest analogous approved source (University of Sydney GI Database sucrose entry and peer-reviewed SSB GI literature) with documented estimation basis. All values are internally consistent and nutritionally plausible.
notes: protein_g is naturally zero as this is a water-sugar-tea-extract beverage containing no protein-bearing ingredients; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central branded RTD tea entries. fat_g is naturally zero by product formulation (no fat-containing ingredients); confirmed zero per product label and USDA Branded Foods. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to 0.0 as this is a fat-free beverage with no fatty acid fractions. trans_fat_g confirmed zero per product formulation. cholesterol_mg confirmed zero — plant-derived beverage with no animal ingredients. dietary_fibre_g confirmed zero — no fibre-containing ingredients in this RTD beverage. potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this specific Fuze Tea Jasmine Green Tea SKU are not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2026-04-16; the product label does not declare these micronutrients. GI of 60 is an analogous estimate based on sucrose GI (University of Sydney GI Database) and peer-reviewed SSB GI literature (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008); no direct GI measurement for this product exists in the University of Sydney database. Cultural ethnic_tags in the input ('Wings', 'Fried Chicken') appear to be erroneous metadata from the GrabFood listing context and have been corrected to reflect the actual product category. Input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) did not directly support the nutrient values; USDA FoodData Central used as the primary approved source.
